title: Stacks & Queues
description: Master stack and queue data structures for efficient operations

documentation.content:
# Stacks & Queues: The Order Keepers

Imagine you're at a cafeteria. The stack of plates works one way—you take from the top and add to the top. But the line of people? That's a queue—first person in line gets served first. These simple ideas power some of the most elegant algorithms in computer science.

## The Story Behind Stacks

A stack is a Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) data structure. Think of it like a stack of books—you can only add or remove from the top. The last book you put on is the first one you'll take off.

**Real-world stacks:**
- Browser back button (last page visited is first to go back to)
- Undo feature in editors (last action is first to undo)
- Function call stack (last function called is first to return)
- Plates in a cafeteria

## The Story Behind Queues

A queue is a First-In-First-Out (FIFO) data structure. Like a line at a coffee shop—the first person in line is the first to be served.

**Real-world queues:**
- Print job queue (first document sent is first to print)
- Customer service lines
- Task scheduling in operating systems
- Breadth-first search in graphs

## Stack Operations

**Push** - Add element to top: O(1)
**Pop** - Remove element from top: O(1)
**Peek/Top** - View top element without removing: O(1)
**isEmpty** - Check if stack is empty: O(1)

## Queue Operations

**Enqueue** - Add element to rear: O(1)
**Dequeue** - Remove element from front: O(1)
**Front** - View front element: O(1)
**isEmpty** - Check if queue is empty: O(1)

## When to Use Stacks

**Parentheses matching** - Check if brackets are balanced
**Expression evaluation** - Convert infix to postfix, evaluate expressions
**Backtracking** - DFS, maze solving, undo operations
**Function calls** - Recursion uses implicit stack
**String reversal** - Push all chars, pop them out

## When to Use Queues

**BFS traversal** - Level-order tree/graph traversal
**Task scheduling** - Process tasks in order
**Buffer management** - Streaming data, IO buffers
**Sliding window** - Maintain elements in window
**Cache implementation** - LRU cache with queue

## Common Mistakes

❌ **Stack overflow** - Pushing too many elements without checking capacity
❌ **Queue underflow** - Trying to dequeue from empty queue
❌ **Not checking isEmpty** - Causes errors when accessing empty structures
❌ **Using wrong structure** - Stack for BFS or queue for DFS won't work
❌ **Inefficient queue** - Using array with shift() is O(n), use circular buffer

## Pro Tips

💡 **Implement stack with array** - Use push/pop methods, they're O(1)
💡 **Implement queue with linked list** - Efficient enqueue/dequeue
💡 **Circular queue** - Use array with two pointers, no shifting needed
💡 **Two stacks = one queue** - Classic interview problem
💡 **Monotonic stack/queue** - Keep elements in sorted order for optimization

## Stack vs Queue Decision

**Use Stack when:**
- You need to reverse order
- You're doing backtracking
- You need to remember previous states
- LIFO order makes sense

**Use Queue when:**
- You need to maintain order
- You're doing level-by-level processing
- FIFO order makes sense
- You're implementing BFS

## Real-World Applications

**Stacks:**
- Browser history (back/forward buttons)
- Text editor undo/redo
- Compiler syntax checking
- Expression evaluation calculators
- Depth-first search algorithms

**Queues:**
- Operating system task scheduling
- Printer job management
- Network packet routing
- Breadth-first search algorithms
- Message queues in distributed systems

Stacks and queues are the building blocks of many complex algorithms. Master these, and you'll see them everywhere! 📚🎯

questions.question: What is the time complexity of push and pop operations in a stack?
questions.explanation: Both push and pop operations in a stack are O(1) because we only access the top element.

questions.question: Which data structure follows FIFO (First-In-First-Out) principle?
questions.explanation: Queue follows FIFO principle where the first element added is the first one to be removed.

questions.question: Which traversal algorithm typically uses a queue?
questions.explanation: BFS uses a queue to process nodes level by level, while DFS uses a stack.

questions.question: What happens when you try to pop from an empty stack?
questions.explanation: Attempting to pop from an empty stack causes a stack underflow error. Always check isEmpty() first.

problems.title: Implement Queue using Stacks
problems.description: Implement a first in first out (FIFO) queue using only two stacks. The implemented queue should support all the functions of a normal queue (push, peek, pop, and empty).
constraints: At most 100 calls will be made to push, pop, peek, and empty
All calls to pop and peek are valid

problems.title: Min Stack
problems.description: Design a stack that supports push, pop, top, and retrieving the minimum element in constant time. Implement the MinStack class with push(val), pop(), top(), and getMin() methods.
constraints: Methods pop, top and getMin will always be called on non-empty stacks
At most 3 * 10^4 calls will be made to push, pop, top, and getMin

problems.title: Valid Parentheses
problems.description:
Given a string s containing just the characters '(', ')', '{', '}', '[' and ']', determine if the input string is valid.

An input string is valid if:
1. Open brackets must be closed by the same type of brackets.
2. Open brackets must be closed in the correct order.
3. Every close bracket has a corresponding open bracket of the same type.

hints.text: Use a stack! When you see an opening bracket, push it. When you see a closing bracket, check if it matches the top of the stack.
Create a map of closing to opening brackets: {')': '(', '}': '{', ']': '['}. For each character, if it's opening, push; if closing, pop and check match.
After processing all characters, the stack should be empty for a valid string.

starterCode.python:
def is_valid(s):
    # Your code here
    # Use a stack to track opening brackets
    pass

problems.title: Implement Queue using Stacks
problems.description:
Implement a first in first out (FIFO) queue using only two stacks. The implemented queue should support all the functions of a normal queue (push, peek, pop, and empty).

Implement the MyQueue class:
- void push(int x) Pushes element x to the back of the queue.
- int pop() Removes the element from the front of the queue and returns it.
- int peek() Returns the element at the front of the queue.
- boolean empty() Returns true if the queue is empty, false otherwise.
constraints: At most 100 calls will be made to push, pop, peek, and empty
All the calls to pop and peek are valid

exampleTestCases.description: Create queue, push 1, push 2, peek (returns 1), pop (returns 1), check empty (false)

hints.text: Use two stacks: one for pushing (input stack) and one for popping (output stack).
When popping or peeking, if the output stack is empty, transfer all elements from input stack to output stack. This reverses the order!
Push is O(1). Pop/peek are O(1) amortized because each element is moved at most once from input to output stack.

commonMistakes: Not checking isEmpty before pop/dequeue - causes underflow errors
Using array.shift() for queue in JavaScript - O(n) operation, use linked list or circular buffer
Forgetting to update both pointers in circular queue
Stack overflow from pushing too many elements without capacity check
Using wrong data structure - stack for BFS or queue for DFS
patternDetection: Problem mentions 'valid parentheses' or 'balanced brackets' - use stack
Need to reverse something - stack is perfect
Level-by-level processing or BFS - use queue
Backtracking or DFS - use stack (or recursion which uses implicit stack)
Need to track minimum/maximum in O(1) - use auxiliary stack
Keywords: LIFO, FIFO, undo, redo, backtrack, level-order, recent, history
